architecture.summary: The paper analyzes sparse autoencoders (SAEs) that decompose language model activations into interpretable features, and studies automated natural-language explanation assignment pipelines. The work does not propose a new system but rather diagnoses a failure mode in existing auto-interpretability workflows: when multiple distinct features receive identical or near-identical textual explanations, the explanation fails to discriminate feature identity. The paper formalizes this 'descriptive collision' problem and proposes corrective scoring metrics (collision-adjusted detection and discrimination scoring) that penalize non-discriminative explanations.

biblio.title: Descriptive Collision in Sparse Autoencoder Auto-Interpretability: When One Explanation Describes Many Features

claims.dataset: Marks et al. (2025) human-annotated SAE features (722 features, Gemma 2 2B and Pythia 70M)
claims.statement: The mean annotation string is reused across 3.07 features

claims.dataset: Marks et al. (2025) human-annotated SAE features (722 features, Gemma 2 2B and Pythia 70M)

claims.statement: The single most common annotation string ('plural nouns') labels 101 distinct features spanning 18 layers and four model components

claims.dataset: Marks et al. (2025) human-annotated SAE features (722 features, Gemma 2 2B and Pythia 70M)
claims.statement: The average annotation resolves only 70% of feature identity
Current detection-style auto-interpretability scoring is invariant to collision
Ignoring descriptive collision inflates reported feature interpretability by a quantity equal to roughly one-third of the bits required to identify a feature
The collision problem is independent of, and additive with, previously identified failure modes of auto-interpretability
Descriptive collision is a complementary, structurally distinct problem to polysemanticity
